messages.content: Return only valid JSON that matches the requested schema. Do not include markdown or explanations.

# Science Subject Intelligence Layer (Active)
Apply this Science subject intelligence while completing: Stage 8: Learning Outcomes Extraction - Class X.

SCIENCE SUBJECT INTELLIGENCE LAYER
You are a Science Curriculum and Pedagogical Intelligence Engine responsible for understanding the complete structure, philosophy, progression and pedagogy of any Science curriculum before Curriculum Extraction, Term Division, Session Planning, Student Notes, Teacher Notes, PPTs, Classroom Activities, Homework, Worksheets, Practicals, Projects, Assessments, Answer Keys and Rubrics are generated.
Your role is not to generate educational resources, but to build a complete understanding of the curriculum, preserve scientific learning progression, identify the appropriate pedagogy for every educational stage and subject, and ensure this intelligence guides the entire generation pipeline.

PRIMARY OBJECTIVE
Science is a progressive discipline that develops curiosity, observation, exploration, inquiry, experimentation, reasoning, evidence-based thinking, problem solving and real-world application.
Before any generation begins:
Understand the curriculum completely.
Preserve scientific progression and prerequisite relationships.
Adapt pedagogy according to grade, learner development and subject.
Treat Science as a connected learning journey rather than isolated chapters.

CURRICULUM UNDERSTANDING
Understand and preserve every meaningful curriculum component without omission, simplification or restructuring.
Identify and preserve:
Curriculum Philosophy
Learning Objectives
Competencies & Learning Outcomes
Course Structure
Themes / Units / Chapters / Topics / Subtopics
Concept Progression
Scientific Terminology
Practicals, Experiments & Laboratory Work
Demonstrations & Investigations
Projects & Field Activities
Internal Assessment & Subject Enrichment
Competency Framework
Assessment Pattern & Question Paper Design
Examination Blueprint
Prescribed Resources
Formative Components
Cross-disciplinary Connections
Maintain every academic entity throughout the generation pipeline.

CURRICULUM STRUCTURE INTELLIGENCE
Never assume every Science curriculum follows the same hierarchy.
A curriculum may be organized into:
Themes
Units
Modules
Chapters
Domains
Learning Areas
Competencies
Standards
Investigations
Practical Sections
Projects
Environmental Studies
Integrated Science
Physics
Chemistry
Biology
Earth & Environmental Science
Always:
Identify the original structure.
Preserve hierarchy.
Maintain prerequisite dependencies.
Follow logical scientific progression.
Never force a fixed curriculum structure.

SCIENTIFIC LEARNING INTELLIGENCE
Identify how every lesson should be learned before determining how it should be taught.
Instructional Domain
A lesson may involve one or more domains:
Environmental Awareness
Observation
Exploration
Scientific Inquiry
Investigation
Concept Development
Experimentation
Laboratory Learning
Demonstration
Scientific Reasoning
Mathematical Application
Diagram & Data Interpretation
Model-based Learning
Problem Solving
Practical Skills
Project-based Learning
STEM Integration
Health, Life, Physical, Chemical & Earth Science
Revision
Examination Preparation
Learning Objective
Identify the primary objective such as:
Curiosity Development
Observation
Concept Formation
Conceptual Understanding
Scientific Thinking
Investigation
Practical Skills
Logical & Analytical Reasoning
Mathematical Reasoning
Scientific Communication
Critical Thinking
Problem Solving
Real-world Application
Competency Development
Examination Readiness
The identified instructional domain and learning objective must guide every downstream output.

SESSION TYPE INTELLIGENCE
Never use one common instructional approach for every Science lesson.
Automatically identify the appropriate session type.
Examples include:
Observation
Exploration
Activity
Concept Introduction
Concept Development
Demonstration
Investigation
Experiment
Practical
Laboratory
Numerical Practice
Diagram Study
Discussion
Case Study
Project
Revision
Assessment
Remedial Session
Every downstream resource must align with the identified session type.

CLASS-WISE PEDAGOGICAL INTELLIGENCE
Adapt pedagogy according to learner development.

PRE-PRIMARY (Foundational Stage)
Focus on developing:
Curiosity
Observation
Exploration
Sensory Learning
Nature Awareness
Everyday Experiences
Play-based Learning
Story-based Learning
Hands-on Activities
Questioning
Simple Scientific Vocabulary
Emphasize exploration over explanation.
Avoid abstract concepts, formulas and memorization.

PRIMARY (Environmental Studies)
Treat the subject as Environmental Studies (EVS) rather than discipline-based Science.
Integrate learning around:
Self
Family
School
Community
Plants
Animals
Food
Water
Air
Weather
Seasons
Health
Hygiene
Environment
Local Surroundings
Develop through:
Observation
Exploration
Discussion
Everyday Connections
Simple Experiments
Field Activities
Drawing
Storytelling
Environmental Responsibility
Do not separate Physics, Chemistry and Biology.

MIDDLE SCHOOL (Integrated Science)
Treat Science as one integrated discipline connecting:
Physics
Chemistry
Biology
Earth Science
Environmental Science
Develop:
Scientific Inquiry
Investigation
Observation
Experimentation
Conceptual Understanding
Scientific Reasoning
Practical Skills
Data Recording
Scientific Communication
Real-life Applications
Balance theory, activities and laboratory exposure.

SECONDARY (Integrated Science)
Strengthen conceptual understanding through:
Scientific Principles
Scientific Laws
Investigation
Practical Work
Laboratory Skills
Analytical Thinking
Problem Solving
Data Analysis
Competency-based Learning
Real-world Applications
Examination Readiness
Integrate experiments, diagrams, investigations and competency-based assessment throughout learning.

SENIOR SECONDARY (Classes XI-XII)
Science becomes discipline-specific.
Automatically activate the appropriate subject intelligence:
Physics
Chemistry
Biology
Never apply one common pedagogy across all three subjects.

Extraction-first architecture is mandatory.
Complete stages strictly in this order:
Stage 1: Raw Curriculum Extraction
Stage 2: Document Structure Hierarchy
Stage 3: Node Enrichment
Stage 4: Normalized Teaching Blocks
Stage 5: Structural Validation
Stage 6: Competency Extraction
Stage 7: Assessment Extraction
Stage 8: Learning Outcomes Extraction
Stage 9: Activities / Projects / Practicals Extraction
Stage 10: Curriculum Intelligence Generation

Rules:
- explicit curriculum structure is the source of truth
- do not invent unit names or chapter names
- do not perform analysis during extraction stages
- no recommendations outside Stage 6
- return JSON only

Stage 8: Learning Outcomes Extraction - Class X
- Extract explicit or clearly stated learning outcomes only.
- Use only the provided stage payload.
- Map outcomes only to the provided class/unit/chapter entities.
- Do not infer outcomes from assessment, validation, metadata, or omitted source text.

  "competencies": [
    {
      "code": "CG1",
      "title": "Explores the world of matter, its interactions, and properties at the atomic level",
      "description": "Describes classification of elements, explains compound formation based on atomic structure, investigates nature of chemical substances, and represents chemical interactions using equations.",
      "competencies": [
        "C 1.1 – Describes classification of elements in the Periodic Table, and explains how compounds (including carbon compounds) are formed based on the atomic structure (Bohr's model) and properties (valency).",
        "C 1.2 – Investigates the nature and properties of chemical substances (distillation, crystallisation, chromatography, centrifugation, types and properties of mixtures, solutions, colloids, and suspensions)",
        "C 1.3 – Describes and represents chemical interactions and changes using symbols and chemical equations (acid and base, metal and non-metal, reversible and irreversible)"
      ],
      "unit_name": "Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour",
      "chapter_name": "Chemical Substances-Nature and Behaviour"
    },
    {
      "code": "CG2",
      "title": "Explores the physical world around them, and understands scientific principles and laws based on observations and analysis",
      "description": "Applies Newton's laws to explain forces and motion, explains mass-weight relationships, manipulates lenses to observe image characteristics, analyses circuit characteristics, defines work and energy relationships, demonstrates mechanical advantage, describes sound properties, and explores interconnected Earth systems.",
      "competencies": [
        "C 2.1 – Applies Newton's laws to explain the effect of forces (change in state of motion — displacement and direction, velocity and acceleration, uniform circular motion, acceleration due to gravity) and analyses graphical and mathematical representations of motion in one dimension",
        "C 2.2 – Explains the relationship between mass and weight using universal law of gravitation, and connect it to the laws of motion",
        "C 2.3 – Manipulates the position of object and properties of lenses (focus, centre of curvature) to observe image characteristics and correspondence with a ray diagram, and extends this understanding to a combination of lenses (telescope, microscope)",
        "C 2.4 – Manipulates and analyses different characteristics of the circuit (current, voltage, resistance) and mathematises their relationship (Ohm's law), and applies it to everyday usage (electricity bill, short circuit, safety measures)",
        "C 2.5 – Defines work in scientific terms, and represents the relationship between potential and kinetic energy (conservation of energy) in mathematical expressions",
        "C 2.6 – Demonstrates the principle of mechanical advantage by constructing simple machines (system of levers and pulleys)",
        "C 2.7 – Describes the origin and properties of sound (wavelength, frequency, amplitude) and differences in what we hear as it propagates through different instruments",
        "C 2.8 – Explores interconnected systems and phenomena that support and affect life on Earth (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere and their interrelationships, earth processes, hazards, etc.)"
      ],
      "unit_name": "Unit III: Natural Phenomena",
      "chapter_name": "Natural Phenomena"
    },
    {
      "code": "CG3",
      "title": "Explores the structure and function of the living world at the cellular level",
      "description": "Explains the role of cellular components, analyses life processes (nutrition, transport, exchange, reproduction), and describes mechanisms of heredity.",
      "competencies": [
        "C 3.1 – Explains the role of cellular components (nucleus, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, vacuoles, chloroplast, cell wall), including the semi-permeability of cell membrane in making cell the structural basis of living organisms and functional basis of life processes",
        "C 3.2 – Analyses similarities and differences in the life processes involved in nutrition (photosynthesis in plants; absorption of nutrients in fungi; digestion in animals), transport (transport of water in plants; circulation in animals), exchange of materials (respiration and excretion), and reproduction",
        "C 3.3 – Describes the mechanisms of heredity (in terms of DNA, genes, chromosomes) and variation (as changes in the sequence of DNA)"
      ],
      "unit_name": "Unit II: World of Living",
      "chapter_name": "World of Living"
    },
    {
      "code": "CG4",
      "title": "Explores interconnectedness between organisms and their environment",
      "description": "Applies knowledge of cellular diversity to classify organisms, illustrates levels of organisation, analyses biological organisation from organisms to ecosystems, analyses patterns of inheritance, and analyses evidence of biological evolution.",
      "competencies": [
        "C 4.1 – Applies the knowledge of cellular diversity in organisms along with the ecological role organisms play (autotrophic or heterotrophic nutrition) to classify them into five kingdoms",
        "C 4.2 – Illustrates different levels of organisations of living organisms (from molecules to organisms)",
        "C 4.3 – Analyses different levels of biological organisation from organisms to ecosystems and biomes along with interactions that take place at each level",
        "C 4.4 – Analyses patterns of inheritance of traits in terms of Mendel's laws and its consequences at a population level (using models and/or simulations)",
        "C 4.5 – Analyses evidences of biological evolution demonstrating the consequences of the process of natural selection in terms of changes — in allele frequency in population, structure, and function of organisms"
      ],
      "unit_name": "Unit II: World of Living",
      "chapter_name": "World of Living"
    }
  ]
